A History Of Penance, Being A Study Of Authorities, Volume 1: The Whole Church To A.D. 450
A HISTORY OF PENANCE
BEING A STUDY OF THE AUTHORITIES
VOL. II
THE WESTERN CHURCH FROM A.D. 450 TO A.D. 1215
CONTENTS
PART B. — THE HISTORY OF PENANCE IN THE WESTERN CHURCHES FROM A.D. 450 TO A.D. 1215
X. FROM A.D. 450 TO A.D. 650
I. THE CONTINENTAL SYSTEM
TEXT OF AUTHORITIES 499
PRELIMINARY SURVEY OF THE PERIOD A.D. 460 TO A.D. 1215 536
After the disruption of the Roman empire the Church proceeds to conquer the conquerors.
Extraordinary variety of use in the exercise of Penance.
The investigation will henceforth be confined to the West.
The Western history of Penance now falls into three divisions : —
I. From A.D. 460 to A.D. 660.
A. The Continental system.
1. Penance public.
2. Reconciliation public.
3. Reconciliation by the bishop.
B. The Keltic system.
1. Penance private.
2. Reconciliation private.
3. Reconciliation by a priest.
Also the practice of recurring confession.
II. From A.D. 660 to A.D. 960. (Period of northern missions.)
Spread of the private system of Penance.
In England public penance never practised.
The Penitential of Theodore.
Continental acceptance of the private system of penance and reconciliation.
Recurring confession becomes a practice of the devout life.
III. From A.D. 960 to the Lateran Council of A.D. 1215.
Development of the practice of confession till it is required of all once a year.
THE PERIOD FROM A.D. 450 TO A.D. 660 538
At about A.D. 460 Penance employed for two classes of persons : —
(a) Those who come forward voluntarily.
(b) Those attainted by authority.
But large numbers of offenders escape all discipline.
Points for inquiry.
Arrangement chronological.
THE "LIBER PONTIFICALIS" 540
Simplicius appoints three supplementary stations at Rome for penitents and Baptism.
VICTOR VITENSIS 541
The Historia Persecutions Provincial Africance.
The persecution of the Catholics in Africa by the Arian Vandals.
Reference to the according of the status of Penance, and also of reconciliation.
S. FELIX III 542
Synodical letter of S. Felix III. indicating grades of penance.
No sign anywhere of these grades in the Roman church.
To be understood as a citation of the Nioene canons.
For young persons the imposition of the hand for a time.
S. FULGENTIUS OF RUSPE 543
Treatise of S. Fulgentius On the Remission of Sins.
No remission outside the Catholic Church.
Sin not remitted here will not be remitted hereafter.
GENNADIUS 544
Gennadius of Marseilles On Ecclesiastical Doctrines.
Those who communicate must be free from the will to sin.
For committed sin there should be tears and prayers.
If the sin be capital there must be public penance, or the monastic life.
The confession of the dying may find the Divine pardon.
Differences of opinion in southern Gaul on this subject.
FAUSTUS OF REII 546
Faustus of Reii is identified with the rigorist contention.
Death-bed penitence insufficient because acts of penance not forthcoming.
Monks exempt from public penance.
S. AVITUS OF VIENNE 547
Case of a man who married his wife's sister.
Counsel that separation of life be accepted without requiring public penance.
JULIANUS POMERIUS 547
The De Vita contemplativa of Julianus Pomerius, professor at Arles.
Confession made to the clergy.
Discipline may be imposed by authority : excommunication to follow default.
Some whose sins are not notorious may discipline themselves by self-inflicted penance and excommunication.
Some communicate without justification.
Alternative courses.
Case of ecclesiastics.
The assumptions underlying the Catholic conception of Penance.
The admission by Pomerius of self-inflicted penance as an alternative was an important departure.
S. CAESARIUS OF ARLES (bp. A.D. 502-A.D. 542) 550
Life of S. Caasarius.
Moral condition of southern Gaul terribly relaxed.
Outward marks of those doing public penance (Agde A.D. 506).
Terms of public penance.
Public penance suited to capital sins.
Priests not to bless penitents in church (Agde).
Disabilities after Penance.
Thus in southern Gaul moral laxity and public penance co-exist.
Result the general use of death-bed Penance.
Caesarius urges penance in the lifetime.
For capital offences public penance.
With confession to the priest.
S. CAESARIUS OF ARLES 555
Yet even for capital sins there is another way.
This is to live worthily through the whole course of life.
Caesarius does not look for public penance from young married men, or from those in military service.
Public penitents might have taken a private course.
The alternative of a bettered life is to be in its way Penance.
It is to produce the fruits of penance in the reconciliation of the sinner.
Pomerius had for such alternative penance contemplated a set term :
Caesarius looks for it to cover the whole course of Life.
Great importance of the admission of this alternative penance.
S. Caesarius gives a careful pronouncement on death-bed penitence.
The acute controversy on this subject in southern Gaul.
There are three kinds of death-bed penitence : —
1. When (a) there are no capital offences, or (b) the capital offences have been washed out by good works. Penance at death is not needed for these : but if rendered, the persons will not only be pardoned but rewarded.
2. When the persons have deferred the Penance till death : but have sinned without the spirit of abandonment, and now purpose amendment.
3. When Penance is deferred till death, and even then restitution and forgiveness are not forthcoming.
For this last class the priest may give Penance, but will be doubtful of the Divine forgiveness.
The prevalent conviction that all do well to seek Penance at death.
Caesarius desired that none should die "without the medicaments of Penance."
Death-bed penitence involved (1) confession to the priest, (2) admission to the status of the penitent, (3) reconciliation by the priest, at least by the Viaticum.
Caesarius teaches that Penance may be accorded twice.
Homily xiii. An enumeration of alternative modes of penance.
Indisposition of the Church to accord penance to young men.
S. GELASIUS {POPE A.D. 492-A.D. 496) 562
Public penance required at Rome for those who had united themselves with consecrated virgins.
COUNCIL OF EPAON (A.D. 617) 563
At Epaon public penance for heretics retained, but reduced to two years.
For cases of homicide canons of Ancyra to be observed.
COUNCIL OF GERONA (A.D. 617) 563
Persons whose penance was not public may be ordained.
Communion is benedictio penitential.
COUNCIL OF BARCELONA (A.D. 640) 563
Observances of public penitents.
Those who receive penance in sickness must, if they recover, lead the life of penitents.
COUNCIL OF ORLEANS (A.D. 649) 564
Excommunication not to be inflicted for trifling causes.
S. BENEDICT OF NURSIA (A.D. 480-A.D. 640) 666
Confession of evil thoughts to be made to the abbat.
VICTOR TUNUNENSIS (d. A.D. 667) 566
Liber de penitentia.
Necessity of confession to a spiritual physician.
Penance may be accorded twice.
Repentance finds pardon, for it is the gift of God.
THIRD COUNCIL OF TOLEDO (A.D. 589) 566
This council reconciled the Arian Goths to the Church.
Public penance enjoined.
Penance only admitted once.
Outward features of the public penance.
S. GREGORY THE GREAT (POPE A.D. 590-A.D. 604) 568
Gregory's theory of priestly absolution.
The priest looses those whom the Lord has already forgiven.
Yet Gregory speaks highly of the dignity of the commission; which, given to the Apostles, is now vested in the bishops.
S. Gregory's teaching on absolution compared with that of S. Leo.
The Church rather with S. Leo, that in absolution the Lord acts with and through His ministers.
Cases in which S. Gregory's view will be held to apply.
Three things required of the penitent : — (1) Conversion, (2) confession, (3) retaliation.
Confession without the fruits of penance is inadequate.
Some confessions inadequately made.
Lent, in S. Gregory's time, had only thirty-six fasting days, and tithed the year.
S. ISIDORE OF SEVILLE (bp. A.D. 600-A.D. 636) 572
Outward features of the public penance.
Grave sinners confess "with the priest standing solemnly in front of them in the presence of God."
Priests and deacons confess their sins to God only.
Three means of remission : — (1) Baptism, (2) Penance, (3) Martyrdom.
Lent has only thirty-six fasting days.
Exomologesis includes confession, penance, and amendment.
S. ELIGIUS (ELOI) OF NOYON (bp. A.D. 641-A.D. 660) 574
Life of S. Eligius.
His confession before a priest in early life.
His connexion with Luxeuil.
His Homilies preached in coma Domini.
Public penance still in force.
Public penitents are first excluded from communion, and then admitted to Penance.
Outward features of public penance.
Absolution given by the bishop on the coma Domini with the laying on of hands.
S. Eligius on absolution.
Eligius, like Caesarius, recognises the Penance of a consistent life.
All Christians such penitents.
Danger of deferring Penance till death.
Lent begins to be related to Penance.
Distinction between public penitents and other persons present.
Penance may be accorded twice.
THE "GELASIAN SACRAMENTARY" 578
Varying antiquity of sections of the Gelasian Sacramentary.
Order ad penitentiam dandam.
Prayers super pomitentes.
Order for those doing public penance.
Ash Wednesday as the caput jejunii.
Penitence conterminous with Lent.
Some accompaniments of the public penance remain.
THE "CANON PSEUDO-AGATHENSIS" 581
History of this canon.
It details the circumstances of public penance at about A.D. 654.
Leading features.
THE "PONTIFICALE ROMANUM" 583
The directions for the expulsion of penitents in the Pontificate
Romanum are closely related to the canon just cited.
The circumstances of public penance are substantially the same.
THE SPANISH SERVICE OF "INDULGENTIA" 585
The Fourth Council of Toledo enjoins the Indulgentia on Good Friday.
The Mozarabic Breviary.
The Liber Ordinum gives an alternative form of the Indulgentia.
The Indulgentia has no distinction of penitents : it is a congregational use.
Those who worthily take part in it may communicate on Easter Day.
XI. FROM A.D. 450 TO A.D. 650
II. THE KELTIC SYSTEM
TEXT OF AUTHORITIES 687
SECOND SYNOD OF S. PATRICK (c. A.D. 475) 603
Penance involves weeping, sad attire, and confinement.
GILDAS (d. A.D. 570) 603
The Proefatio de Penitentia of Gildas.
It registers the discipline in force in the British church in the sixth century.
In this discipline penances are private without public counterparts in the Liturgy.
And there is no solemn reconciliation by the bishop.
The Prcefatio is a monastic code, but covers all Church action for the graver sins of monks.
Confession is presupposed.
S. DAVID 604
Synod of Llandewi-Brefi {ante A.D. 569).
Synod of Lucus Victoria (A.D. 569).
The records of these synods are brief penitentials.
They are primarily of monastic scope.
Excerpta quasdam de Libro Davidis.
These not mainly for monks.
No reference to penitential exercises in the Liturgy, or to solemn reconciliation by the bishop.
What was the character of the confessions noticed?
The mission of S. David, S. Gildas, and S. Cadoc to Ireland.
S. FINIAN OF CLONARD 606
The Poenitentiale Vinniai.
The lives of S. Finian show discrepancies and anachronisms.
The Poenitentiale may be most fitly assigned to S. Finian of Clonard.
Clonard a monastery of the Irish type.
It contains monks, clerks, students, penitents.
The non-monastic inmates.
The Irish communities combined the functions of a monastery with those of an university.
Eminence of the Irish scholarship in the sixth century.
The penitents of the Irish monasteries.
Some are inmates of the monastery; others not.
The abbat's relation to the penitents. He had a sphere of influence, which included : —
(a) Inmates : monks, clerks, scholars, penitents.
(b) Externs : country-folk, visitors.
S. FINIAN OF CLONARD 608
Within his sphere the abbat exercises supreme jurisdiction.
Incomplete character of the Poenitentiale Vinniai.
The saint's statement as to the Penitential.
Some of the provisions of the penitentials of S. Finian and S. Columbanus are shown side by side.
Various penances.
The features which stand out are seen to be these : —
1. Penance has not the public character.
2. Reconciliation has not the public character.
3. Reconciliation is not effected by the bishop.
Nor in the Penitential of S. Finian is it clear that the priest pronounces any formal absolution.
S. COLUMBANUS 612
The Penitential of S. Columbanus.
Training of S. Columbanus.
He and twelve other monks set out for the Continent.
The motive is mortification rather than missionary zeal.
Anagrai, Luxeuil, Fontaines.
These are all monasteries of the Irish type; and reproduce the various classes of inmates.
Columbanus exercises supreme jurisdiction.
After twenty years he is driven forth. His foundation at Bobbio.
The Regula Monachorum, the Regula Cenobialis, and the Penitential.
The Penitential of S. Columbanus represents the Keltic system, with the exception of § B. 25.
Its contents.
It is not confined to monastic persons.
Various penances. These are privately performed.
The reconciliation also has no public character.
The priest is much more in evidence than in S. Finian 's penitential.
§ B. 25 prescribes public penance, and public reconciliation by the bishop for certain heretics.
It is due to some Gallic source outside Columbanus.
The injunction of diligent confession before mass.
This is, in fact, a monastic instruction.
Confession enjoined in the Regula Camobialis.
S. Columbanus does not seem to have extended his system of Penance to persons outside his bounds.
S. EUSTASIUS 620
His successor, Eustasius, did.
S. DONATUS (A.D. 592-A.D. 651) 621
S. Donatus founder of the monastery of S. Paul at Besancon,
And is in authority over the convent of Joussamoutier.
His rule for Joussamoutier based on S. Caesarius, S. Benedict and S. Columbanus.
Confession to be made assiduously to the abbess.
REVIEW OF THE FOREGOING EVIDENCE REGARDING THE KELTIC OR PRIVATE SYSTEM 621
EXTENSION OF THE KELTIC SYSTEM ON THE CONTINENT 622
Next to be noticed is the further extension of the Keltic system on the continent of Europe.
(A) MONASTERIES MULTIPLIED 622
First through the remarkable multiplication of monasteries on the model of Luxeuil.
Irish founders : S. Gall, S. Fridolin, S. Fursey.
Frankish foundations on the Keltic model.
Decline of the ancient Gallic monasteries.
(a) MONASTERIES MULTIPLIED (CONTINUED) 623
Foundation of Soliznac by S. Eligius.
Jouarre, Reuil, Rebais, Faremoutier, S. Valery, Centule, S. Vandrille, Jumieges.
The penitential system of S. Columbanus in force in all these houses.
The attraction of the monastic life lay largely in its provision for Penance.
(b) LEADING MEN OF KELTIC TRAINING 625
Next to be noticed are the prominent men of Keltic training.
S. Eligius. His confession to a priest in early manhood.
No hint here of public penance.
His own public reconciliations as a bishop. Yet his personal sympathies with Luxeuil.
Bishops of Luxeuil training : Chagnoald, Acharius, Ragnacharius, Omer, Faro, Ouen, Remaclus, Hildebert, Dieudonne.
Saints; twenty-one from Luxeuil in twenty years.
Episcopal sympathy with the private system of Penance.
Council of Chalon, between A.D. 639 and A.D. 654.
Penance is a medicine of the soul useful for all men.
The priest may impose penance.
The abbat Adso on the influence of the foundations of Columbanus.
(c) THE PENITENTIALS 627
The penitentials put forth on the continent in the seventh century.
Each penitential is a handbook of the private system of Penance for the use of the priest.
The Pomitentiale (pseudo-)Romanum and others.
Their characteristics.
The introduction of the P. pa. -Romanum giving directions for the conduct of the private system of Penance.
This is the first-known directory for the priest's office in private penance.
Of the penances of the P. pa. -Romanum twenty based on Columbanus, six on Gildas, others from various sources.
The penances for homicide.
This penitential certainly produced in the Frankish lands.
A peculiar feature of the P. pa. -Romanum are the two offices taken from the public use : (a) for giving penance, (b) for reconciling the penitent.
These offices derived in great part from the Roman SacramentArles.
Hence the name P. Romanum.
The phrase ex acrinio Romance ecclesias due to Halitgar.
The P. Bobiense and the P. Parisiense.
The vogue of the private or Keltic system at about A.D. 650.
XII. FROM A.D. 650 TO A.D. 950
I. TO CHARLES THE GREAT
TEXT OF AUTHORITIES 632
PRELIMINARY SURVEY OF THE PERIOD A.D. 650-A.D. 950 643
The period A.D. 650-A.D. 950 is the period of the conversion of Northern Europe, and in particular of the English peoples.
In England the missionArles were (a) Roman, (b) Keltic.
In England public penance and public reconciliation by the bishop never found actual observance.
The private system of Penance accepted by Theodore.
In England also the practice of recurring confessions for persons in the world takes its beginnings.
On the continent the Keltic (private) system is employed in monasteries of Keltic origin.
PRELIMINARY SURVEY OF THE PERIOD A.D. 660-A.D. 950. 644
And with it the practice of recurring confession.
The influence of English missionArles and English men of letters.
The clash of the public and private systems.
The reform councils of A.D. 813.
By A.D. 960 the private system, and recurring confession, have laid hold of all Western Christendom north of the Alps.
ENGLAND 646
The ancient Keltic Christianity and the continental mission side by side in England.
The Northumbrian Church Keltic.
S. Cuthbert.
Mercia is converted from Northumbria.
The missions of S. Felix in East Anglia, and of S. Birinus in Wessex.
Wilfrid in Sussex.
Theodore's statement that "in this province" there was no public penance, and no public reconciliation.
Theodore's life. His appointment to Canterbury. His character and work.
The Penitential of Theodore
Was not written by Theodore, but gives his pronouncements;
Was taken down by Eoda the priest;
And edited for all English Catholics by the discipulus Umbrenaium.
Subjects treated in the Penitential of Theodore.
His summary of contemporary practice at Rome and in the East.
Theodore admits the private system, and reconciliation by a priest.
What Keltic book was in the hands of Eoda?
Examples of penance prescribed in Theodore's penitential.
Confession may be made to God alone.
Later English penitentials (Poen. Boedas; Poen. Egberti).
The beginnings of the practice of recurring confession.
Monastic practice.
Statement of the Dialogue, of Egbert that annual confessions had been employed in the church of the English from the times of Vitalian and Theodore.
Importance of the introduction of recurring confession.
It is adopted first in a missionary region.
THE CONTINENT OF EUROPE 666
The development of private penance on the continent.
This may be traced in four different streams : —
1. The development of the beginnings set in course by S. Columbanus.
2. The influence of the Penitential of Theodore.
3. The English missionArles.
4. The English scholars at the court of Charles the Great.
Othmar, abbat of S. Gall. His instructions for the making of a confession.
The influence of the Penitential of Theodore.
The variety of penitentials.
English missionArles: S. Boniface, S. Willibald, S. Willehad, S. Willebrord, S. Lioba, S. Walpurga.
S. Boniface.
It is better to confess to one man.
The German council of A.D. 742. The only priests to attend the army to be chaplains for the hearing of confessions.
Commutation of penance.
Case of Adalbert.
Bavarian council between A.D. 740 and A.D. 750. The people not to shrink from confessing their sins in the presence of the priests.
Statute qucedam formerly attributed to Boniface.
Priests to reconcile penitents immediately after their confessions.
THE CONTINENT OF EUROPE 663
The Regula canonicorum of S. Chrodegang.
The clergy had formerly not been admitted to Penance.
S. Chrodegang, bishop of Metz (A.D. 742), founder of Canons regular.
He adapts the rule of S. Benedict for his canons.
They are to confess yearly, or twice a year, to the bishop or his deputy.
The rule thus derived from monastic practice, in fact, synchronises with the spread of the private system of Penance among the laity.
In future no distinction in procedure will be discernible between clergy and laity.
XIII. FROM A.D. 650 TO A.D. 950
II. Charles the Great and After
TEXT OF AUTHORITIES 665
THE TIMES OF CHARLES THE GREAT. ALCUUIN 688
Alcuin (A.D. 735-804).
Eminent as a man of letters.
Head of the palace school at Aachen.
Abbat of S. Martin at Tours A.D. 796.
Letter "to the brethren in the province of the Goths."
Sketch of Gothic Christianity.
The laity do not practise confession to the priests.
Opusculum to the students of S. Martin's at Tours.
Exhortation to all the students to use confession.
The same treatise sent to Arno, archbishop of Salzburg, for his students.
To the same Arno that the clergy should exhort to confession.
Advice to one of his "sons" to use confession.
To Damoetas to prepare for a campaign by confession.
Letters to monastic communities.
(a) Wearmouth. To wash away sins by confession.
(b) Fulda. The students to use confession.
(c) Monasteries throughout Ireland. None to decline confession.
All this the more modern teaching.
THEODULF 693
Theodulf of Orleans, bishop and poet (died c. A.D. 821).
Born in Spain of Gothic race.
At the court of Charles.
Modifies the Gothic position, which declined confession.
Accepts confession to a priest as open to a Christian.
The CapitulArles of Theodulf.
Distinguishes between confession to a priest and confession to God alone. Both admissible.
When confession is made to a priest it must be adequate.
The eight principal vices.
Lenten exercise of penance.
Clergy to prepare themselves for hearing confessions.
Penances to be sought in the canons of the Fathers and in Holy Scripture.
Thus originates the position of the anti-penitential party.
Theodulf has place both for public and for private penance.
Theodulf perhaps the latest writer who teaches that what calls for public penance is capital sin.
Henceforth the teaching will be that public offences call for public penance, and that private offences are met by private penance.
PUBLIC ENACTMENTS 697
No imperial requirement of confession from the people.
Capitulary of A.D. 802. All priests to be prepared to assign penances.
Another Capitulary of A.D. 802. Inquiry to be made as to how priests hear confessions and impose remedies.
No mention in these CapitulArles of penitential books.
But episcopal inquiries call for knowledge of a penitential.
SURVEY OF WESTERN CHRISTENDOM 698
All the Western Christendom of the continent now comprised in the empire of Charles, except the province of the Popes.
The two streams of fervour from the British Isles, (a) Keltic, (b) English.
Charles the Great. A true spiritual reformer.
THE COUNCILS OF A.D. 813 700
The reform synods of A.D. 813 at Mainz, Rheims, Tours, Chalon, and Arles.
The independent action of Charles in spiritual matters.
Contrast of the public and private systems of Penance.
Council of Arles. Public crime to be met by public Penance.
Council of Chalon.
The emperor to be asked to assist in requiring public Penance.
Confession may be made to a priest, or to God alone.
If to a priest, instruction to be given as to the eight principal vices.
Penitential books to be repudiated and altogether banished.
Penance to follow (1) Canons, (2) Scripture, or (3) Custom.
The Council of Chalon follows the lead of Theodulf
Council of Tours. Desiderates an authoritative penitential.
Council of Rheims.
Priests to know how to receive confessions and assign penances.
Public penance to be retained for those requiring it.
Council of Mainz. Incestuous persons to be expelled.
The Capitulary of the assembly of Aachen A.D. 813,
Accepts (1) expulsion of the incestuous, and (2) imposition of public Penance.
Gives no ruling as to penitentials.
Capitula ecclestaetica (c. A.D. 813); these leave over the question of penitentials.
CONTINUANCE OF THE CONTROVERSY ABOUT PENITENTIALS 707
Council of Paris (A.D. 829). Penitentials to be burnt.
Confession regulated : — (a) Absolution after penance, (b) Penance according to canons, (c) Reconciliation only on episcopal authority.
Jonas, bishop of Orleans.
It is the custom to confess the graver sins : few confess the minor sins.
Vernacular forms of confession in Germany. The Fulda form (c. A.D. 830).
NEW COMPILATIONS 708
Ebbo, archbishop of Rheims, calls upon Halitgar, bishop of Cambray, to compile a penitential based upon Fathers and Canons.
Halitgar adds to his compilation the Pamitentiale Romanum.
The phrase ex acrinio Romane ecclesie.
Otgar, archbishop of Mainz, similarly calls on Rabanus (Maurus), then abbat of Fulda, to make a selection from Canons and Fathers.
Rodulf, archbishop of Bourges (c. A.D. 850),
Compiles a guide for priests from Fathers and Canons.
Other collections.
Council of Mainz (A.D. 847). Penances from (1) Canons, (2) Scripture, (3) Custom.
NEW COMPILATIONS 710
Public penance for public sin (following Rabanus).
Rodulf of Bourges repeats this.
So too Hincmar, archbishop of Rheims (A.D. 857).
Council of Mainz (A.D. 852).
Private penance admitted for secret incest.
Change in character of penances imposed.
The ninth century is thus a period of conflict as regards Penance.
The new compilations not felt to be an improvement on the penitentials.
PENITENTLALS REGAIN POSSESSION BY A.D. 900 713
Regino, abbat of Prum.
De synodalibus causis et disciplinis ecclesiasticis (c. A.D. 906).
Priest required to have "a Roman penitential, either that put forth by Theodore the bishop, or by the venerable Bede."
The penitentials have thus regained possession by A.D. 900.
Recommends confession at the beginning of Lent.
Even for those who have not committed mortal sins.
DISCRETION OF THE PRIEST 714
Council of Worms (A.D. 868).
Recognises the discretion of the priest.
So too the Council of Tribur (A.D. 895).
LOMBARDY 714
Sketch of Lombard history.
Lombardy under Charles not a part of the Frankish realm.
It has its own laws and traditions, both civil and ecclesiastical.
The monastery of Bobbio, founded by S. Columbanus, is a centre of Keltic traditions.
Here, no doubt, the private system of Penance would find observance.
Elsewhere in Lombardy no evidence of private penance.
Pauhnus of Aquileia.
Letter to Heistulf, a Lombard who had killed his wife (A.D. 794).
